title: 2025/07/16 - SSC (Part 2)
displayTitle: 2025-07-15 - Social Services and Community Committee - Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, Oranga Tamariki: Inquiry into procurement and contract management [Part 2]
topic: Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, Oranga Tamariki: Inquiry into procurement and contract management
description: Wednesday, 16 July 2025 | Social Services and Community Committee Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, Oranga Tamariki: Inquiry into procurement and contract management Hearing of evidence Oranga Tamariki - Andrew Bridgman, Chief Executive (Acting) - Benesia Smith MNZM, Deputy Chief Executive (Commissioning and Investment) - Jesse Roth, General Manager (Commissioning and Investment)
descriptionSummary: Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, Oranga Tamariki: Inquiry into procurement and contract management. Oranga Tamariki. Andrew Bridgman, Chief Executive (Acting)

chunks.text: Good morning and welcome to the Social Services Community Select Committee. Uh, so look, I've wanted to take an opportunity to, um, introduce yourself and your team, and then, um, some opening comments on understanding of a PowerPoint presentation that your team, uh, has to, uh, present to us. And once we've moved through that, we'll move the questions from there. Sure. Um, thank you very much, chair. Um, look, my name is Andrew Bridgeman and I'm the acting Chief Executive of and, um, Venicia Smith here, who's the Deputy P Chief Executive of Commissioning

and Investment, um, and Jesse Roth, um, who, uh, is, um, works in the commissioning, um, and investment group, um, who's been intricately involved with, um, with the processes. Um, but what I thought I'd do is just, uh, start with some brief opening remarks, um, that kind of look at the past probably, and then hand over to Benicia to give, uh, an in-depth sort of PowerPoint that I think will explain where we want to go to, um, partly a result, a result of the OAG report.

So really to try to give the select committee confidence that we've heard them, uh, we acknowledge the issues and we want to do something about it. I think that's the, the critical thing here. Um, so look firstly, um, uh, to reiterate that we accept the findings from Deloitte and, uh, um, Ernest and Young and the Auditor General reports.

Um, and we want to continue to strengthen how we work with our partners to support the needs of Tamika and children. So that's kind of a clear, um, uh, platform. Uh, our focus is firmly on insurance services are in place for those we are responsible for supporting. Our role is to make sure Tam and anthi and hanau have what they need to feel safe, nurtured, and to flourish.

Um, and we always obviously do this within the context of a budget. Um, so we are probably no different to health and other big systems. Um, ultimately, um, things get rationed because you're working with unlimited, probably demand and limited supply. Um, we are committed to changes needed to improve the procurement process, and you'll hear about that very, very shortly.

Um, I think it's important to note that before the RIG inquiry commenced, we had Aang, tamari had already instigated, um, an an internal review, um, which was undertaken by Ernest and Young because we were worried about the feedback that we were getting in that, uh, process. Based on those findings, we had already begun to make changes, including creating a new leadership role to enable a dedicated focus on procurement, improving provider engagement and refining internal systems.

And that's the role that Benicia has taken on. Um, look, we want to acknowledge that our past communication and engagement with providers was inconsistent and in some instances inadequate. So we just wanna put that on the table. We are taking more time and care with decisions and strengthening how we communicate and engage, um, with our partners and providers.

And again, you'll get a good sense of that from Isha. Um, we know that trusted transparent relationships are critical to success, and we are really intending to build on that. Um, I want to acknowledge to all providers, sorry, I have acknowledged to all providers, and I've written to those providers, the deficiencies in our engagement during 24 25, um, the contracting round and the communication that providers and staff were provided wasn't adequate.

So I just wanna let you know I've done that in writing and also I've done that in a number of forums, um, uh, um, media forums, um, and then has made changes to my, um, second tier and organization as a result of that to make sure that we have the right focus and heft on, on, uh, on, on commissioning and investment.

Um, so, um, I probably just really wanna summarize by saying that, um, we want to ensure that the services that we provide are effective, equitable, and delivered in a way that makes the Graves needs times change. And so do the needs of the families. And that's one of the tricky things with this, is that contracting is never set forever.

It really depends on what are the children need at any one time. Uh, we know we have legacy programs and initiatives that are no longer fit for purpose, uh, and that ministerial priorities and organizational objectives change over time. So contracting needs to reflect that. Our improvements and procurement are about focusing our core role on our core role in ensuring the right support in place for those that need it most.

So look, I'm just gonna leave it at that and then hand over to Benicia to talk about, um, uh, the way forward and what we are doing now to address those issues outta the ig. Thank you. Thank you, Thank you. Anyways, uh, we, we will have from the presentation go questions afternoon, so questions after the entire presentation?

Yeah, yeah. Not as she's on. Okay. Thank you. Just getting to, Uh, Tina Koto. So, um, we're gonna get into the presentation that you've got in your pack with a bit more detail. Um, there's probably four key things I would say before I get into the presentation. The first thing is that, um, uh, public trust and confidence, um, in the commissioning and investment function, um, uh, um, uh, needs to be rebuilt and kind of recognize that that takes time.

It's a journey, um, to do that. And that's what this plan, um, is underpinned by, is about rebuilding trust and confidence. The second thing I would say is, um, and building trust and confidence. You need to be really clear around what your approach is, your processes and timeframes.

And so we have an approach that is best based on best practice, uh, domestically and internationally that we need to be transparent about, have timeframes, um, and we'll talk a bit about that. Uh, the third thing is, um, in order to deliver that, we need good internal systems and processes that are operating efficiently and effectively, which includes, um, our staff, uh, clearly understanding their core roles and responsibilities and having clear communications.

And again, we'll talk about that. And then the fourth thing is that this plan has been predicated on ensuring that we are responding to, uh, the reviews that have been undertaken of order order. So particularly the Deloits s Young and the orders general report. Thank you. We we're just having a little issue with the clerks and trying to get the presentation online so members have in front of 'em.

And if you want to work through while we try and resolve that technical issue. Okay. So, um, we have developed a proposed plan. It is proposed because we are currently engaging across the motu, um, and seeking feedback. It is centered on four key programs, fixing 24, 25, refreshing our approach, uh, resetting, um, and refining how we undertake services, rejuvenating outcomes and engagement approach.

And as I said, it's about, um, being underpinned by the reviews that have been undertaken. Now, the first program of work, um, is, has been a, a core focus for Orang since I arrived in March, which has been looking at what happened in 24, 25, and making sure that we understand what contracts were in place, what contract variations were in place or not in place.

Mm-hmm. So, so, um, in March there was about a hundred contract variations that were outstanding, and we spent, uh, a good couple of months getting that sorted, uh, so that by the end of the financial year, all 554 providers, um, had contracts in place.

Um, and, um, yeah, so that's the first thing. The second thing that we, uh, spent quite a bit of time on also was, uh, making sure that, um, the minister's announcement, which was in January this year around extending any contracts or services that ended on the 30th of June, 2025, were extended for six months.

We identified that there were 194 providers, and, um, by the end of 30 June, 2025, all of those contract sections were out. And I think about 180 of them were back. We were waiting for about 15, and that's the first time in seven years that we've ever had one a year actually in place and contracts in place before the financial year actually starts the, the year of, of delivery.

Um, do we, where would you like, um, Clarkson to take you to? Oh, Jenny. Yeah, Jim, we're on prop one. So, um, yeah, so when I think about the checklist of the kind of four kind of key things around the reviews, um, that have come up, and it's around making sure we've got strategic procurement and planning errors based decision making, contract management and relationship secret and governance program.

One has been focused in on making sure our contract management and relationship integrity is being focused on. Um, and I needed to do that so that we could get into, uh, this financial year with a clean slate from a contract management perspective.

Program two, um, is about resetting our, um, approach. Now, as I said before, um, this approach is actually based on best practice, um, in New Zealand. So NSD follow a very similar process. So does SAA education, corrections justice, um, and internationally, um, in Australia, Canada and uk, they, they're very similar kind of social commissioning approaches, uh, going through kind of these broadly five steps.

Unfortunately, um, we hadn't been using the steps, um, at, um, in a coherent and consistent way. We had at times missteps and other times we'd significantly compressed them. So, um, and this whole process really does start with assessing regional needs and understanding in the regions what our children, young people and whanau need, um, that have come to our attention.

And making sure that the investments, uh, um, purpose in a way that meets the, the regional needs. So we need to go through that process. Um, we need to then, once we've undertaken that needs analysis, then go through a process of understanding what services, uh, should continue, what services need to be, uh, decommissioned and repurposed, um, and what services are not required anymore.

And we need to do that regularly because needs do change. Um, and you know, an example of that is, um, in Auckland, um, we we're investing in quite a few programs around teen parenting. The numbers around teen parenting has significantly decreased over the last, you know, five years.

So we have decommissioned those programs and reinvested that money in other services, uh, in the Auckland region. And we need to be doing that regularly because needs in the regions do change. So that's what we need to be doing. We also need to be thinking about, um, um, if there are new services required, um, and what procurement process we need to go through and how do we source.

Now, when I think about these steps, steps one to three really should be started 15 months prior to the year that you're going to start delivering the services at a minimum. I have had some feedback already from regional engagement. Um, and a worry is about step three in particular, which is the sourcing providers and negotiating contracts, particularly when it's new services and new money is required.

And it's due to the conundrum of the cap of the budget process. Obviously the budget process happens, um, and decisions are made basically the second Monday of April, uh, budget sensitive until budget day. That means I wouldn't be able to start a, if I had been successful in the budget process procurement until after May.

Um, and so the timeframe of delivering services from one July for new services and new funding doesn't work. Um, so I think we'd had to change the timeframe for that and, and look at undertaking a procurement process and possibly having services, um, delivered from maybe one February to kind of leave about six months to go through the procurement process and, and negotiate the contracts.

So it's just something that I need to reflect, um, uh, in the, in, in this, um, approach as a result of regional engagement. The other comment from regional engagement to date has been around, there's two other kind of key co components for you to know is on step one, uh, there's been discussion about having more time for regional needs assessment, uh, possibly starting that kind of March-ish, you know, maybe late February.

And then the third broad comment to date, um, is thinking about, um, what is the key, um, principles that underpin the approach around transparency, those types of making them very clear and and transparent. Um, and, um, and also being really clear about what are the core priorities.

Um, so, um, that's just an update of this core. So that's kind of the steps, uh, that we need to undertake. Um, and, um, I think kind of moving on to program two, program two, um, is all about refreshing our approach. We've got kind of a weird year this year for 25, 26.

It's a bit weird because of the 194 contracts have only been, um, extended for six months, which is, um, uh, so that means we need to work out what happens for the next six months. Um, we have to report back to cabinet on that, and they'd like us to report back in August.

So after regional engagement on what our proposed, uh, strategic approaches to commissioning, uh, what our funding requirements are beyond 31 December, uh, are they, uh, do we want to decommission anything? And if so, fully or partially, I can tell you right now it won't be fully 'cause we've done six months.

Um, and I think the other thing, uh, they want us to come back to them on is any services or programs that might be proposed for transition to, uh, the social investment agency, um, more broadly. If I was undertaking that approach that I've just outlined, the five steps, it would mean, um, uh, earlier this year, kind of in March, April, may, we would've been, um, undertaking, uh, regional engagement to discuss regional needs.

We'd be analyzing them, kind of going through step two, the end of step two. I'm proposing that we would report, uh, back to the minister in about November of a year to kind of say the year ahead. This is what it's looking like. We've identified that these are the regional needs, these are the services that need to continue. These are the services that we think need to be decommissioned and repurposed that, uh, investment and, and these services, uh, need to be decommissioned and let's work on a exit and develop an exit

and a transition plan, uh, with those providers around, uh, what that means. Um, and so in relation to program two, there's a range of things that, you know, we need to focus our attention on. Again, going back to the reviews, we need to be making sure that we are making strategic procurement, um, uh, decisions.

So that needs to be based on our approach, what our priorities and strategy is. Uh, we need to be making sure that we have a plan and that it's transparent, that people understand what the plan is, just kind of why the five steps in a proposed timeframe so that organizations, our partners and providers can kind of plan for it. Uh, and we need, it needs to be evidence-based decision making.

So again, that's why we need, uh, good data and information and our regional needs assessment undertaken, have good contract management, um, practices in place and making sure that we are, uh, continuing to engage and, um, uh, and, and rebuilding trust and, and, and confidence.

And we need to have good kind of internal governance arrangements in order to make this happen. So that's kind of program two, program three is around how we do things internally. And there's a, a range of things that we need to be focusing our attention on. We need to be, um, thinking about our contract framework.

We currently utilize the MB social sector contract framework, but we've made a number of modifications to it, so of our colleagues at other social sector agencies, and we need to be looking at terms and conditions and seeing what we can do to be, um, more consistent with, um, other agencies.

And if we're being different, outlining very clearly our rationale for why we have different terms and conditions, which is not clear at the moment. Uh, we also need to be thinking about our contract rotation. So we have 554 providers that provide over 700 services in a year, across three years.

We, uh, look at a third of them. So about 170 ish I can add, yeah, ish a year. And, um, but currently there's no rationale for why it's that 170. It's not done by services, it's not done by program, or it's not done by region. So we need to realign our rotation process.

If you were looking at needs within a region and services, um, you know, across a couple of regions, you'd want to make sure that your contract rotation was aligned. And currently it's not. So that's gonna take a bit of time to kind of unpick, but it is something we need to focus our attention on, and we need to significantly step up our game around data collection and reporting.

In many instances, we have, um, you know, outcome agreements, but they have output measures. So they do things like, count the number of beds and ask us about your utilization rates. And that's the wrong question. I mean, I do want to know that they have 17 beds, but I actually want to know about what's happening with the 17 tiki that have been in those beds.

Are they warmer? Uh, now that they're warmer and they're eating, that means that they might be going to school, that they've been to the doctor, that they've had a dentist appointment. That's the data that I want in addition to the very output things. So we don't ask the right questions. And, um, set in my original engagement today, actually, I be sure the questions you have that you, you know, that or, or actually answers that you want to be giving us, and we are just not letting you.

So let's look at how we can improve our data collection. We also do collect quite a lot of data and we don't share it. So there are other agencies, um, that I've looked at. Um, if I just look at my colleagues actually at the Ministry of Justice, they do this great dashboard report back, I'd like us to move into doing some of that dashboard reporting so that providers can see, you know, the data that's coming in, see what it's saying.

We currently don't do that, um, kind of at a regional level or at a, um, or at a national level regularly, or if at all. So I kind of wanna get into that type of reporting also. Um, so, uh, I think the other thing, uh, that I wanna make sure internally is that we've got good guidance and support, and that we have, um, we're really clear around our internal processes and that we are being efficient.

We're not having a process that has slightly exaggerating 57 steps. I kind of know that things need to be cut out. Um, so that's kind of program three, oh, I'm going through this quickly. Program four, um, is, um, around rejuvenating our comms and engagement.

So we kind of went through a period where we didn't communicate and engage well from national office, and we, uh, need to change that. So one of the things is around, um, setting up our provider. So since I've been back on board, we've done three, um, that provide a range of information and respond to questions that have been raised.

Um, and I want to do that more regularly. Um, we're also doing regional engagement. So to date, we've had 12 of 21. We've also had engagement with strategic partners through, uh, national providers and with the, so through the Social Services Providers association.

Uh, and you're just wanting to do more of that. I, in the plan, I'm proposing at least two times in a year that would be coming out that does not in any way take away from the ongoing regular engagement that I expect to be happening at the regional level. Um, and, and maintaining, um, those relationships and making sure that they're healthy.

I wanna have a look at, you know, whether we should think about some online hui, because my regional engagement is probably gonna be in quarters one and three. So to date. And actually this is as a result of engagement today, they said you should do online engagement benicia, and in quarter two and four. And I'm very open to doing that. They also have suggested I'm like, yes, we can make sure that it happens, is that we are using, um, current communication channels.

So SSPA is a good, a top pay order. They have a great online facility. Um, Belinda, the chief executive said, yes, Carpay, you can utilize that. Um, but if there are other communication channels that are trusted, then I want to make sure that we are, um, uh, using those instead of setting up new channels.

So this is all around our contract management and relationship integrity. Um, and kind of the next slide, uh, basically outlines kind of a four year horizon around, um, this process and kind of implementing, um, or resetting the commissioning and investment function.

What you'll see kind of in out years is that if the commissioning approach that step one to five is work is operating optimally, that we would be, um, working on two years simultaneously within a financial year. But we would back at the beginning of, you know, 15 months ahead.

And we'd be kind of at the end of, um, another one, which I mean is we would be, um, uh, delivering services, monitoring, you know, those types of things. Um, you'll however, see that this year, so the calendar year of 2025, we are actually going to have, um, three years running.

Uh, yeah. And it's 'cause we are still dealing, you know, we've been dealing with 24, 25. Yes. We're also dealing with 25, 26. And in my regional engagement, I have commenced regional needs assessment mahi with them, um, at the regional Hui. So we've started 26, 27. So the first corridor all is happening in the regions around what data is needed in order to, um, and what is the data saying, uh, so that we can then, uh, come back to them in kind of October going, this is what we think the regional needs needs assessment

says and test it. So that's kind of the program of work. Um, there's, uh, the stars indicate kinda report backs to kind of minister's cabinet, uh, processes. Um, but I feel like there's a, a real opportunity, uh, to build on, uh, the recommendations from the reviews, um, to build back trust and confidence to regularize, um, the commissioning and investment approach and way it operates because it can be regularized.

Um, um, but it is underpinned though by making sure that we understand what is required in the regions. And we're taking both a regional approach, assessing needs, looking at is our investment in the right places and making adjustments when we need to, and just being really transparent about that.

Um, but in a way that we, uh, making sure that the, the social sector, the NGO social sector is coming along with us. Mm-hmm. So shorter. Well, thank you very much. Um, KA put A hand up first. Thank you very much, um, for that. There's some good stuff happening. Um, we appreciate it.

Um, I just wanna start with, um, the auditor general's findings, that statement that quote, some providers have been able to accumulate significant surpluses, and this is unacceptable end quote, and other media releases was not based on sound evidence, unfairly harmed providers and undermined trust in the agency.

Uh, you opened by saying, um, I think you said you accept the findings of the report. So does Oranga TAM stand by that public statement today? Well, I think what we've acknowledged is that, um, and this is what this process is about, we acknowledge the Iigs findings.

Um, we acknowledge that, um, we've got more work to do when we kind of look at the balance sheet when an organization, for instance, when they have under underspends, um, to find out, um, exactly what is the nature of those underspends and why have they occurred. I mean, our critical interest is if if we were funding an organization, whatever, it's a hundred thousand to deliver to 10 tamari, um, and there was an underspend, did that mean because they're only delivering to six tamari, which means what do we do about, you know, that,

that that that sort of gap. So, and it's, uh, I think going forward with the time that we're talking about, you know, so that kind of 15 month period, um, that we are looking at, um, that will also mean that we'll get involved. You know, I think it'll be a much more of a conversation. I think the critical thing is that we want a fund for services that are provided and for whatever reason, if those services aren't provided, because there's not the demand, where is the demand in that region to reallocate the could.

The flip side of that though, is there'll be times when the demand exceeds. So we've gotta be nimble enough to say, you know, if we've projected a certain demand over the year that demand exceeds in the first six months, what do we do then about if we can, addressing that increase in demand? And I'm not saying we guaranteed to do it because again, but like the health system, you've, you're kind of rationed in one sense, but, but hopefully we'll have a much better understanding.

We can kind of, we can respond to that. So I just had four quick supplementaries. So has issued or considered issuing a clarification or retraction of its media release in light of the oags findings? And if not, why not? Well, um, no. I mean, at the end of the day, we've acknowledged the findings, and to be frank, the, the, the, the, the burning, the burning focus has been, we've got, you know, we've got a, um, EY report, we've got an OIG report before the OIG report even came in, you know, I mean,

I've been in this job since mid of September, right? Mm-hmm. So, you know, there is a problem, clearly there is a problem. What do we do about it? And my analysis of it was we had, well, part of it was we had a, a big, a group responsible for two big areas, procurement and devolution. Devolution is huge in another itself. So I thought, okay, we're gonna split, we're gonna split those groups.

I'll bring in someone that just primarily focuses on procurement, and then I've asked Benicia, we need a plan, and their plan needs to be rigorous enough that addresses those issues out of OAG. And, um, uh, um, uh, ey, I've, I've acknowledged our acceptance of the IIG recommendations, um, in, in Hui face to face and on Zoom hui to literally hundreds of people.

And I've acknowledged it by leader. So that's, that's just been, that's been the focus. Thank you. Um, so just following through, through on that, um, I will just read the quote that came from that press release or that media, um, release. It was from, in previous years, some providers have been able to accumulate significant surpluses, and this is unacceptable.

Every cent of funding we give providers needs to be reaching the children and young people for which it was intended not sitting idle in the individual bank accounts. So, can officials explain what analysis supported this claim that provided surpluses were unacceptable? And was any engagement actually undertaken with those affected providers before that judgment was made public?

Well, I'll, I'll do the first part, perhaps Jesse, do the second part. I mean, it was, you know, really based on, um, you know, that first part is quite legitimate, isn't it? I mean, at the end of the day, we, we, we are providing people money to deliver a service. So our role, and, and you have a, as interest in this as we do, is that money doesn't sit idle and that it is, uh, delivering on the services that the, there's nothing wrong with that.

But perhaps if, if you want to talk briefly about the, what the rationale entail. Sure. So, um, so very, uh, briefly, um, you know, we did note some irregularities in the STEM profile of the number of agencies and significant accumulation of some surpluses.

Yeah. Uh, but we acknowledged that we didn't take the steps to engage with those providers appropriately to understand the issues, um, contributing to those surpluses. Thank you. Um, just two more. Um, supplementaries, what processes are now in place to ensure future communications during these sensitive procurement processes are accurate, fair, and do not generalize provider performance?

Because in that response, I was a bit concerned that yes, you have stated that you accept the findings, but then rereading the actual quote that was made, you're sort of standing by that. Um, and you would've read that, read it to, to show that that surplus didn't necessarily come from funding.

So that is a bit concerning, but I do want to look forward and see how we actually, um, accurately and fairly, um, assess it and not generalize the provider performance. Yeah. So look, I, I will hand over to Isha to answer that, but, but perhaps to start off with, look, absolutely agree.

Um, you know, each is an individual contract and we've gotta focus specifically on the, the, the issues facing the, the individual entity in terms of the funding that's with them, their spend rate, what they've left over, et cetera. Um, so totally agree with that. Um, but Anisha, in terms of going forward, do you want to Mm, I'd just probably emphasize that we are in the process of resetting and that it's gonna take time and it's why I'm out, um, in the TU talking directly to providers and that they're not just receiving a letter

from me or from Andrew. And it's about rebuilding our relationships with them, recognizing that many of them do have quite strong relationships, uh, with our regional staff. So it's, uh, reinforcing that that relationship is still really key, and that I'm gonna be working extra hard to make sure that I'm communicating really well with my regional people so that they have all of the right messages.

And, um, and Qs. And as I've also indicated to providers that if they've got issues, they can just literally get in contact. They've got my email address actually already had few emails and kind of respond like, yes, and I need to make sure I'm responding really timely. But, um, and I've just said it will take time.

I, um, there might be times when, um, I'm not timely, but I'm gonna tell you and I'm just gonna communicate. I wanna make sure that we are just being transparent and open as much as possible. Thank you. And this is my last supplementary on this, um, question here. So given the minister has stood by her own, uh, cash cow comments, is Ang Tamari concerned that continuing to defend its own, or, um, not wanting to retract its own damning statements risks entrenching that distrust, especially with providers whose task it is

to keep children safe and away from danger? Look, I, I think there are two parts to this picture. Um, and, you know, uh, the first part is, uh, we've been quite with the, the Hui that Isha was undertaking in the, the, the meetings that we've had is to acknowledge what happened in the past, uh, um, um, and to acknowledge the kind of the processes and the communications were not what they should have been.

Um, so we've been very, very clear about that. Uh, and we've, and we wanted to do that because until we did that, then, you know, you're not gonna, you're not gonna, um, you're not gonna address everyone's anxiety, but at least you're open about it and honest about it. And then the second step is what is the plan going forward?

So, um, my sense would be that, um, uh, it's been, yep, last year was bruising. Uh, people will still feel bruised, but actually they're really focused on what are you gonna do about it and what does the next year look like? And we've been really honest, um, about, um, this is the plan.

We, we've got, we want to engage with you, we want to get your feedback. We, we, it's not gonna be perfect. So there's, there's still gonna be noise. You are still gonna get noise. Mm-hmm. But we hoping the noise will be less, uh, and it will be less, it will be less in volume and less egregious in substance. So, um, and we think these, uh, people are responding to that positively.

Thank you, Ary, Helen. Great. Thank you. Um, look, I think I am hearing you clearly that you acknowledge that that was not a good, uh, a kind of approach to attack the organizations in that way, and that you're be rebuilding trust. And what I heard from Isha, which was really interesting, was this issue about best practice from overseas and also picking up the MB contract model.

But looking at where you needed to differ, if I am correct, that you were trying to build a very different model. No, no, no. I wouldn't say I'm building a different model. It's just that all social sector agencies use that model. And every single social sector agency in the government Yeah.

Has modified it has, he has schedules. So from a provider perspective, there will be a provision about data and reporting that says Apples from one agency and says from another agencies and says, chairs from another agency.
